L’affaire Heidegger
Martin Legros 03 octobre 2012
« Calomnie ! Diffamation ! Ignominie ! » Ces mots ponctuent les réponses, passionnées mais très argumentées, que ce volume collectif apporte au discrédit jeté sur l’œuvre et la personne de Heidegger. On se souvient de l’écho public et médiatique qu… * * *

Martin Heidegger et le vieux pont de bois
Pauline Théveniaud 21 septembre 2012
En comparant une centrale électrique et un pont de bois, le philosophe allemand s’interroge, dans « La question de la technique », sur l’action de l’homme sur la nature. * * *

title: Martin Heidegger - Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy
excerpts:
Consistently throughout his career, Heidegger was an
anti-individualist about meaning—that is, he argued that the
primary disclosure of being is social in character. In Being and
Time , it is “the anyone-self” who “articulates
the referential context of significance” in terms of which
entities are encountered (SZ 129), and “prescribes that way of
interpreting the world and being-in-the-world” (SZ 129). After
the war, it is neighborhoods and localities of mortals who disclose
the sense of things by dwelling together while gathered by the
fourfold. But for a time in the 1930s Heidegger argues that the
disclosive “we-self” is a people ( Volk )
that shares a history, a soil, blood, a language, tasks,
possibilities, moods, and a destiny. Like the Nazis, Heidegger held a
racist and anti-Semitic conception of the essence of the German
people, although he rejected the Nazi’s biologistic conception
of race.

title: Question Concerning Technology, and Other Essays, The: Heidegger, Martin: 9780061319693: Amazon.com: Books
excerpts:
Section Title: Question Concerning Technology, and Other Essays, The Paperback – January 1, 1977 > Martin Heidegger
Content:
Brief content visible, double tap to read full content.
Full content visible, double tap to read brief content.
Born in southern Germany, Martin Heidegger (1889–1976) taught philosophy at the University of Freiburg and the University of Marburg. His published works include: Kant and the Problem of Metaphysics (1929); An Introduction to Metaphysics (1935); Discourse on Thinking (1959); On the Way to Language (1959); Poetry, Language, Thought (1971). His best-known work is Being and Time (1927).

title: Martin Heidegger — Wikipédia
excerpts:
Section Title: Sommaire > Notoriété et influence > Réception aux États-Unis
Content:
L'influence combinée de Hubert Dreyfus , avec son étude de 1991 sur *Être et Temps* , *Being-in-the-World: A Commentary on Heidegger's "Being and Time", Division 1* , et les très nombreux articles de Richard Rorty compilés la même année dans *Essays on Heidegger and Others: Philosophical Papers* , entraînent un foisonnement des études sur Heidegger dans le domaine universitaire, du moins « parmi ceux élevés dans la tradition empiriste » . Parmi eux, Blattner cite Charles Guignon, Mark Okrent, Taylor Carman <sup>[[ 237 ]]()</sup> .
La traduction en anglais de l'ouvrage posthume *Apports à la philosophie : De l'Avenance* , qui parait en 1999 sous le titre *Contributions to Philosophy (From Enowning)* , est considérée comme la seconde œuvre d'importance de Heidegger <sup>[[ 3 ]]()</sup> .

title: Resultados de la Web
excerpts: The utterance of thinking is a telling silence.' Such utterance corresponds to the most profound essence of language; which has its origin in silence. As one in touch with telling silence, the thinker, in a way peculiar to him, rises to the rank of a poet; yet he remains eternally distinct from the poet, just as the poet in turn remains eternally distinct from the thinker.Everything in the hero's sphere turns to tragedy; everything in the demigod's sphere turns to satyr-play; and everything in God's sphere turns to ... to what? "world" perhaps?( *Nietzsche* , by Martin Heidegger, Volume Two, "The Eternal Recurrence of the Same", pp. 201-8, ed. by David Farrell Krell)

title: Martin Heidegger and Perspectives on Death – Dr. Absher's Philosophy 210 Blog
excerpts:
Section Title: Martin Heidegger and Perspectives on Death
Content:
“Every Da-sein must itself actually take dying upon itself. Insofar as it ‘is,’ death is always my own.”
My relationship to the death of others is fundamentally different, according to Heidegger, from my relationship to my own death.
Just as the They transforms the death of others into a curiosity, so too it tranquilizes our relationship to our own death. As I suggested in my previous post, the the anticipation of one’s own death gives rise to *Angst* . But we have the tendency to flee *Angst* and immerse ourselves in the public interpretation of the world and of ourselves in order to avoid the burden of existence. This flight is evidenced in the public interpretation of one’s own death in which it is rendered into an unavoidable, yet distant, event. As Heidegger puts it,
“One also dies at the end, but for now one is not involved.”

title: Understanding Heidegger on Technology — The New Atlantis
excerpts:
Only a rediscovery of being and the realm in which it is revealed might save modern man. In his later writings on technology, which mainly concern us in this essay, Heidegger draws attention to technology’s place in bringing about our decline by constricting our experience of things as they are. He argues that we now view nature, and increasingly human beings too, only technologically — that is, we see nature and people only as raw material for technical operations. Heidegger seeks to illuminate this phenomenon and to find a way of thinking by which we might be saved from its controlling power, to which, he believes, modern civilization both in the communist East and the democratic West has been shackled. We might escape this bondage, Heidegger argues, not by rejecting technology, but by perceiving its danger. Heidegger’s Life and Influence
T he son of a sexton, Martin Heidegger was born in southern Germany in 1889 and was schooled for the priesthood from an early age.

title: Heidegger, Martin | Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy
excerpts:
Heidegger, Martin | Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy
Heidegger's main interest was ontology or the study of being. In his fundamental treatise, Being and Time, he attempted to access being (Sein) by means of phenomenological analysis of human existence (Dasein) in respect to its temporal and historical character.
